---
title: "Prune"
subtitle: "Most weights don't matter. Find which ones do."
description: "A browser mini-game from MLSys Playground. Click weights in a neural network to remove them while keeping accuracy high. Teaches magnitude-based pruning and the lottery-ticket hypothesis."
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="/assets/games/common.css">
          <script src="/assets/games/common.js" defer></script>
          <script src="/assets/games/prune.js" defer></script>
---

```{=html}
<div class="mlsp-game-container" role="region" aria-label="Prune mini-game">
  <canvas id="mlsp-canvas" class="mlsp-game-canvas" width="640" height="420"></canvas>
  <div class="mlsp-game-hud">
    <span class="mlsp-score">accuracy <span id="mlsp-acc">100.0</span>% · sparsity <span id="mlsp-spa">0</span>%</span>
    <span>click faint weights · <kbd>R</kbd> to retry</span>
  </div>
</div>

<div id="mlsp-aha-slot"></div>

<script>
(function mountPrune(){
  function boot() {
    if (!window.MLSP || !MLSP.games || !MLSP.games.prune) {
      return setTimeout(boot, 30);
    }
    var canvas = document.getElementById("mlsp-canvas");
    var $acc = document.getElementById("mlsp-acc");
    var $spa = document.getElementById("mlsp-spa");
    var ahaSlot = document.getElementById("mlsp-aha-slot");
    var api = MLSP.games.prune(canvas, {
      onScoreChange: function(s) {
        $acc.textContent = s.accuracy.toFixed(1);
        $spa.textContent = s.sparsity.toFixed(0);
      },
      onGameOver: function(s) {
        MLSP.showAhaCard(ahaSlot, api.ahaLabel, api.ahaText);
      },
      onRetry: function() { window.location.reload(); }
    });
  }
  boot();
})();
</script>
```

## What you're feeling

The scene on the canvas is a small fully-connected neural network: 5 inputs → 8 hidden neurons → 3 outputs, with every pair of adjacent-layer neurons connected by a weight. Faint edges are small-magnitude weights; bold edges are large-magnitude weights. You're pruning by hand.

The trick you develop after a few rounds: **magnitude is a noisy proxy for importance**. Cutting the faintest 50% of weights usually barely hurts accuracy — those weights really were redundant. But every round seeds the network differently, and a handful of low-magnitude weights are secretly load-bearing. Good players learn to read the *shape* of the network, not just edge opacity.

## Why this matters in real ML systems

Deploying a trained neural network at scale is expensive — every weight costs memory, bandwidth, and energy on inference. **Pruning** removes weights that contribute least to model output, shrinking models 5×–20× with minimal accuracy loss. The [lottery-ticket hypothesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.03635) goes further: it claims that dense networks contain sparse sub-networks which, trained alone, match full-model accuracy. Finding those winning tickets is an active research area; magnitude pruning is the crudest, cheapest heuristic — the one you just played.

---

*Part of [MLSys Playground](/games/) — small games that make ML systems constraints playable.*
